text: This is a training video detailing techniques for laparoscopic small bowel anastomosis. Surgical approach to small bowel resections varies with the underlying abnormality, which can affect placement of the trocars depending on the area of focus. There are a few principles regarding performing laparoscopic small bowel anastomosis. The end-to-end anastomosis restores more accurately the natural continuity of the bowel, but a side-to-side anastomosis is most commonly performed.

Entry into the peritoneal cavity can be performed by what is most comfortable to the surgeon, but trochars should allow for identifying the area of concern, lysis of adhesions, creation of a mesenteric window, stapling of the bowel, division of the mesentery of the abnormal segment of the bowel, reanastomosis of the bowel, and closure of the mesenteric defect. The standard operating room setup for SBR is the surgeon, most often on the right, and the first assist on the left. Ports depend on where larger instruments, such as the stapler, will be used. The surgeon's left hand is most often a five millimeter trocar, and the right hand is a twelve millimeter trocar, in order to use the stapler and suturing device. Usually a two-oh polysorb on SH needle, cut to six inches. The assistant left hand is a camera, and the right hand is a five millimeter port.

The surgeon identifies the proximal and distal ends of the abnormal bowel segment. The mesentery is held perpendicular to the instrument to create a mesenteric window for bowel transection. A 2.5 mm linear stapler is used to divide the small bowel through the mesenteric window. The mesentery of the specimen is then divided using an electrosurgical device.

The surgeon places a traction suture at the anti-mesenteric side of the bowel to align the two portions before re-anastomosis. Hook electrocautery is used to create openings in the bowel lumen, taking care to avoid injury to the back wall of the bowel.

The surgeon uses a 60mm long, 2.5mm linear stapler to reestablish intestinal continuity, ensuring the stapler is fully placed within the bowel lumen and the bowel is evenly aligned. The stapled suture line is inspected for bleeding, which is controlled with interrupted sutures if present. A proximal stitch is then applied.

The surgeon closes the common enterotomy using a running stitch with absorbable suture, ensuring minimal tissue handling. A target suture is placed to aid dexterity, and the closure is performed in a single layer. Alternatively, a stapled closure is executed in a Heinecke-Mekulitz orientation, where the enterotomy is closed transversely.

The video shows the completed suture line alongside the staple line. A stay suture is placed at the end of the mesenteric defect, followed by a running suture to close the remainder of the defect, concluding at the previously placed stay suture.
